conflict_type: Conflicts between Keyword Constraints and Phrase Constraints
seed_instruction: Write an article about why the dinosaurs went extinct.
org_constraint: Include the phrase "a cataclysmic event" in the third sentence.
new_constraint: Avoid using the keyword "cataclysmic" in the article.
instruction: Write an article about why the dinosaurs went extinct. Include the phrase "a cataclysmic event" in the third sentence. Avoid using the keyword "cataclysmic" in the article.
response:
## The Great Dying: Unraveling the Mystery of Dinosaur Extinction

For millions of years, the Earth was ruled by giants. Dinosaurs dominated the terrestrial landscape, evolving into creatures of incredible size, diversity, and power. Their sudden disappearance, approximately 66 million years ago, remains one of the most profound mysteries in paleontological history, marking a dramatic turning point in life on our planet.

The prevailing scientific theory points to a massive impact event as the primary driver of this global upheaval. Evidence strongly suggests that a large asteroid or comet struck the Earth near the Yucatán Peninsula. This impact triggered a series of devastating environmental changes, constituting a world-altering occurrence.

The immediate aftermath of such a colossal collision would have been catastrophic. Massive tsunamis would have swept across coastlines, while the sheer force of the impact would have generated immense earthquakes. More globally, the vaporization of rock and the subsequent injection of vast amounts of dust, soot, and sulfur dioxide into the atmosphere would have initiated a prolonged "impact winter."

This atmospheric disruption had severe consequences for the global climate. Sunlight would have been blocked, leading to a rapid and drastic drop in global temperatures. Photosynthesis, the foundation of almost all life on Earth, would have been severely curtailed as plant life withered under the perpetual darkness.

The collapse of the plant food chain would have cascaded up through the ecosystem. Herbivores, unable to find sufficient vegetation, would have starved, followed closely by the carnivores that preyed upon them. While some smaller, more adaptable species managed to survive by burrowing or finding sheltered niches, the large, specialized dinosaurs were ill-equipped to handle such rapid and extreme environmental stress.

In conclusion, the extinction of the dinosaurs was not likely due to a single factor, but rather a perfect storm of environmental pressures. The massive extraterrestrial impact initiated a chain reaction of climate collapse, rendering the world inhospitable to the dominant reptilian life forms, paving the way for the rise of the mammals that would eventually lead to our own species.<turn|>
